preamble: As part of Grimm's Law, Proto-Indo-European voiceless plosives became voiceless fricatives in Proto-Germanic.

examples.note: Historical fricativization of initial *p. E.g., Proto-Vietic *pa → Vietnamese 'ba' [ɓa] (three) - wait, ba is b. 'phi' [fi] (to fly) from Middle Chinese or similar.
Free variation: /p/ and /f/ are often in free variation or act as allophones for many speakers (e.g., 'sapos' ~ 'safos' meaning 'if').
Yiddish deaffricated MHG initial /pf/ → /f/ word-initially. E.g., MHG pfeffer → Yiddish fefer (pepper), MHG pflanze → Yiddish flants (plant). Medially/finally /pf/ → /p/.
Proto-Semitic *p → Arabic /f/ in all positions; Arabic has no /p/ phoneme. Cf. also Hebrew פָּרַשׁ pārash vs. Arabic فَارِس fāris (horseman).
Latin pater, Greek patēr retain the [p].
